era_context: The Roman Republic is entering civil war. Julius Caesar, having conquered Gaul (58–50 BCE), is ordered by the Senate (dominated by Pompey's faction) to disband his army. Caesar refuses and crosses the Rubicon River in January 49 BCE, effectively declaring war on the Roman state. Pompey the Great, the Senate's champion, flees to Greece to gather forces. The civil war between Caesar and Pompey will dominate the next few years. In Egypt, Ptolemy XIII and Cleopatra VII are in conflict over the throne. Parthia remains the dominant eastern power.

events.title: Caesar crosses the Rubicon; beginning of the civil war
events.description: In January 49 BCE, Julius Caesar, having been ordered by the Senate to disband his army, instead crosses the Rubicon River (the boundary between Gaul and Italy) with his legion. This act is a declaration of war against the Roman state and against Pompey's faction in the Senate. Caesar's famous words, 'Alea iacta est' ('The die is cast'), mark the point of no return. The crossing of the Rubicon becomes a metaphor for an irrevocable decision. Caesar's action triggers the civil war that will last until his victory at Pharsalus in 48 BCE.

sources.name: Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Civili (Civil War), Book I

events.certainty_note: Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon is one of the most famous events in Roman history. The date (January 49 BCE) is secure from multiple sources. Caesar's own account is contemporary.

events.title: Pompey's flight to Greece; gathering of anti-Caesar forces
events.description: After Caesar crosses the Rubicon, Pompey and the Senate's faction flee Italy. Pompey establishes himself in Greece and begins gathering forces from across the Mediterranean world. He recruits senators, equestrians, and soldiers loyal to the Republic. Pompey's strategy is to gather a larger army and confront Caesar in Greece, where he has more support. The flight of Pompey and the Senate marks the collapse of the Republic's political institutions and the beginning of open civil war.

sources.name: Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Civili, Book I

events.certainty_note: Pompey's flight to Greece is well-attested. The date (early 49 BCE) is secure.

events.title: Caesar's rapid conquest of Italy; consolidation of power
events.description: After crossing the Rubicon, Caesar moves rapidly through Italy, securing cities and recruiting soldiers. Most of Italy falls to Caesar without significant resistance; Pompey's supporters either flee or are overwhelmed. By the end of 49 BCE, Caesar controls most of Italy and is preparing to pursue Pompey to Greece. This rapid consolidation of power demonstrates Caesar's military superiority and the weakness of Pompey's position in Italy.

sources.name: Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Civili, Book I

events.certainty_note: Caesar's conquest of Italy is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Egyptian succession conflict; Ptolemy XIII vs. Cleopatra VII
events.description: In Egypt, Ptolemy XIII and his sister-wife Cleopatra VII are in conflict over the throne. Ptolemy XIII, supported by his advisors, has exiled Cleopatra and is ruling alone. Cleopatra is gathering support and preparing to challenge her brother's rule. This internal conflict will eventually draw in Julius Caesar, who will arrive in Egypt in 48 BCE and support Cleopatra. The Egyptian succession dispute is a consequence of the Ptolemaic dynasty's internal instability and the absence of a strong central authority.
events.certainty_note: The Egyptian succession conflict is attested in sources, but the exact timeline and details are less clear. The conflict is dated to around 49–48 BCE.

events.title: Parthian stability under Orodes II; continued dominance of the East
events.description: Orodes II maintains firm control of the Parthian Empire. The Parthians continue to dominate the Levantine frontier and control key trade routes. Roman-Parthian relations are tense but not actively hostile. The Roman civil war between Caesar and Pompey does not directly affect Parthia, but it does distract Rome from eastern affairs and may provide opportunities for Parthian expansion.
events.certainty_note: Orodes II's reign is well-attested; specific events in 49 BCE are not detailed.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon is sometimes given as January 10 or January 11, 49 BCE, but the exact day is uncertain. The famous quote 'Alea iacta est' is attributed to Caesar by Suetonius, but it may be apocryphal or paraphrased. Some modern scholars debate whether Caesar's crossing was truly an act of war or a political maneuver that escalated into war. The significance of 49 BCE is primarily the crossing of the Rubicon and the beginning of the civil war; other events are consequences of this action. The Egyptian succession conflict is less well-documented and may have begun earlier or later than 49 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 49 BCE is rich, particularly for Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon and the beginning of the civil war. Caesar's own Commentarii de Bello Civili is a primary source, though it is written from Caesar's perspective and may be biased. Multiple later sources (Plutarch, Appian, Dio Cassius, Suetonius) provide additional details. The record is heavily weighted toward Rome and the Mediterranean; events in Parthia and other regions are sparse. The narrative is shaped by pro-Caesar propaganda (in the Commentarii) and by later sources that emphasize the drama of the civil war. The Egyptian succession conflict is less well-documented and is known primarily from later sources.

graph_edges.note: Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon led to the Battle of Pharsalus in 48 BCE
Pompey's flight to Greece led to the confrontation at Pharsalus
Caesar's consolidation of Italy enabled him to pursue Pompey to Greece
The Egyptian succession conflict eventually drew in Caesar
